name: M142 HIMARS
detail: High Mobility Artillery Rocket System. US supplied 40+ units starting Jun 2022. 70km range with GMLRS rockets; up to 300km with ATACMS. Transformed Ukraine's ability to strike Russian ammunition depots, forcing Russia to move logistics 80km+ from the front.

name: ATACMS (MGM-140)
detail: Army Tactical Missile System. Range ~300km. First delivered secretly in limited numbers Sep 2023 (used against Crimea). Full authorization provided in 2024. Launched from HIMARS; used to strike Russian airfields, ammunition depots, and military formations in occupied territory and Russia itself.

name: Storm Shadow / SCALP-EG
detail: Anglo-French long-range cruise missile (~250km range). UK supplied from May 2023; France (SCALP) followed. Adapted for use on Soviet-era aircraft then F-16s. Used to strike the Kerch Bridge, Russian HQs in Crimea, and rear-area logistics. Significant escalation beyond prior weapons supplies.

name: Patriot PAC-3
detail: Advanced US air defense system. 3 batteries supplied to Ukraine from 2023. Successfully intercepted Russian Kinzhal hypersonic missiles in May 2023 — first confirmed Kinzhal shootdown. Critical for protecting Kyiv and major cities. Each battery costs ~$1B; interceptors ~$4M each. Ukraine lobbied repeatedly for more batteries.

name: F-16 Fighting Falcon
detail: 4th-generation multirole fighter. Denmark, Netherlands, Belgium, and Norway pledged transfers; first aircraft operational Aug 2024. Pilots trained in Europe for over a year. Improved Ukrainian strike capability and air-to-air combat. Limited numbers (40-80 total) and maintenance complexity mean not a decisive game-changer but significant upgrade.

name: FGM-148 Javelin
detail: US-Swedish fire-and-forget anti-tank missile. US supplied thousands pre-war and post-invasion. Credited with destroying hundreds of Russian tanks in early 2022 Kyiv battle. Ukrainian troops could fire and immediately take cover due to top-attack fire-and-forget capability. Became iconic symbol of Ukrainian resistance.

name: NLAW (MBT LAW)
detail: Swedish-British Next-generation Light Anti-tank Weapon. UK supplied over 5,000 NLAWs pre-invasion and in early 2022. Short-range disposable system ideal for urban warfare. Highly effective in Kyiv battle; light enough for individual soldiers to carry. Became symbol of UK-Ukraine defense cooperation.

name: Leopard 2 (various variants)
detail: German-made main battle tank. Germany, Poland, Canada, Spain, Norway and others supplied ~100+ Leopard 2s after Jan 2023 authorization. Limited operational impact vs. entrenched Russian positions; several destroyed by mines and drones. Better suited to maneuver warfare; Russia's defensive lines negated their advantage. Germany led Western tank coalition.

name: M1A1 Abrams
detail: US main battle tank. US pledged 31 tanks in Jan 2023; delivered Sep 2023. Reportedly withdrawn from front lines by late 2024 due to vulnerability to Russian drones and loitering munitions — logistical complexity also a factor. High fuel consumption and maintenance demands reduced battlefield availability.

name: IRIS-T SLM
detail: German-supplied medium-range air defense system. Germany delivered first battery Oct 2022, becoming the first modern Western air defense in Ukraine. Range ~40km; intercepts cruise missiles, drones, and aircraft. Germany supplied multiple batteries. Particularly effective against Shahed drone attacks. Complementary to Patriot's longer range.

name: NASAMS (National Advanced Surface-to-Air Missile System)
detail: US-Norwegian medium-range air defense. US provided 12 NASAMS systems to Ukraine. Uses AIM-120 AMRAAM missiles. Highly effective against cruise missiles, drones, and aircraft. Central to Ukraine's layered air defense network protecting major cities.

name: Shahed-136 / Geran-2
detail: Iranian-designed loitering munition produced in Russia as Geran-2. $20-50K per unit — much cheaper than missiles. Low-speed subsonic attack profile makes them easier to intercept but challenging in mass swarms. Russia launches 50-130+ per night against Ukrainian cities. Production rate estimated 300+ per month in Russia by 2025. Key weapon in Russia's energy terror campaign.

name: Kh-47M2 Kinzhal
detail: Russian air-launched hypersonic ballistic missile. Putin called it 'invincible.' Ukraine's Patriot system shot down multiple Kinzhals in May 2023 — undermining Russia's propaganda. Range ~2,000km; launched from MiG-31K and Tu-22M3. Russia has limited stockpiles (~50-100 estimated). Used selectively against hardened targets.

name: Lancet-3 Loitering Munition
detail: Russian-made loitering munition with TV/optical guidance. Highly effective against Ukrainian artillery pieces, armored vehicles, and air defense radars. Production scaled to hundreds/month by 2024. Has destroyed numerous HIMARS, howitzers, and other critical Ukrainian systems. Operates in tandem with Zala recon drones — one locates, one strikes.

name: R-360 Neptune
detail: Ukrainian-developed anti-ship cruise missile. Used to sink Russia's Black Sea Fleet flagship Moskva (Apr 2022) — the most significant naval loss of the conflict. Based on Soviet Kh-35 design but substantially upgraded. Two Neptune missiles fired; both struck Moskva. Further strikes forced Russia's Black Sea Fleet to retreat from Snake Island and western Black Sea.
